Roll 01 — 35mm
January 2026
5 frames
Frame 01
After everything was over
35mm · Jan '26

I took this photo after everything was over. After the ceremony, after the portraits, after all the moments that were supposed to matter most. Her bouquet was on the ground, her shoes kicked off, her clutch slightly open like it had been set down without thinking. Nothing was posed anymore. Nothing was being performed.

I stood there for a second longer than I needed to.

I met her freshman year. She met him freshman year.

Back then, life felt contained to smaller things — classes, schedules, friendships that were just beginning to take shape. Marriage wasn't something that belonged to us. It was something we watched from a distance, something reserved for people who had their lives figured out in ways we didn't.

And now she's married.

Not in a way that feels shocking or rushed, but in a way that makes sense. That's what I can't stop thinking about. It makes sense. It fits. There's nothing about it that feels out of place.

Which is what makes it feel so unreal.

At some point, without noticing, we became the kind of people this happens to. The kind of people for whom marriage isn't a distant idea, but a real, possible next step. Some people would look at us now and say we're grown.

I don't know if I agree.

02
Frame 02
I have seen this bench a billion times
35mm · Jan '26

I have seen this bench a billion times. It's always been there, something I pass without thinking, something that just exists in the background of the Arb. But this time it was covered in snow.

It was storming when we went. Not the quiet kind either. The kind where the snow hits your face, sticks to your clothes, makes everything feel heavier. I went with my best friend — the person who has, without even realizing it, replaced so many of my bad memories of Nichols Arboretum with good ones.

But I'm the kind of person where memories stick. Not all of them. Just certain ones. Very specific ones that never seem to leave.

Like when my brother told me I was too stupid to get into Michigan. Or when my mom told me I was becoming a bad daughter because I moved out. Or when my cousin told me he was proud of me right before he died.

This bench holds one too.

It holds a version of me that isolated herself from everyone who mattered for someone who was quietly dimming my soul. I sat on this bench freshman year, but I don't think I ever really saw it.

This time, I saw it. I saw a younger version of myself sitting on it on a warm Friday morning, three years ago.

I stared at the bench for a good minute. Probably longer. Just standing there in the cold, letting everything come back, but not in the same way it used to. It didn't feel as heavy. It didn't feel like something I was still stuck in.

I just stood there, in the freezing Michigan weather, proud of myself.

03
Frame 03
If that isn't girlhood
35mm · Jan '26

These photos were taken at a Michigan basketball game. Nothing particularly special about the game itself. No big rivalry, no defining moment, nothing that would make it memorable to anyone else.

But it felt special to me.

I was there with two other student photographers, all of us switching between lenses, tracking plays, trying to catch the perfect shot in the middle of something fast and loud and constantly moving. One second I'm focused on freezing a moment mid-air, the next second, we're talking about setting spray.

"Your skin is always flawless." And I didn't think twice before going into my entire routine, step by step, product by product, like it mattered just as much as anything else we were doing there.

And it did. Because if that isn't girlhood, then I don't know what is.

The way we can move so easily between worlds. From something high-pressure and technical to something soft and familiar without even noticing the shift. The way those conversations happen in between everything else, casually, like they've always been there.

Senior year has made me realize how important that is. It made me realize how rare it is to have friendships that feel that easy. So innocent and unequivocally loving, that it can't really be explained.

04
Frame 04
We ended up at Pizza House at midnight
35mm · Jan '26

We ended up at Pizza House at midnight.

It was one of those nights where the cold didn't feel real until you stepped outside. The kind that hits your face all at once, makes your eyes water, makes you walk faster without saying anything. We were freezing the entire way there, laughing through it, complaining, pulling our jackets tighter around us like that would somehow help.

When we got inside, everything felt warmer than it actually was.

We ordered pancakes and tater tots, like we always do. One plate with chocolate chips, one plain, both covered in syrup that kept spilling over the edges. The table was crowded — plates, forks, napkins we barely used, phones lighting up every few minutes.

It wasn't a special night. We were just there because we had nothing better to do.

We talked about everything that felt important at the time. Drama in our lives, messy friendships, future careers, families, love lives — it felt like everything.

I remember looking up in between conversations and just taking it in. The way we were sitting. The way nothing felt rushed. The way it all felt so normal, like a moment we didn't need to hold onto because there would be so many more like it.

I still took photos.

The next morning, I woke up to a message in our group chat. A link. "Body found of UMich student reported missing."

I clicked it without thinking. "19-year-old… was last seen walking alone… on the 1700 block of Hill Street near Washtenaw Avenue… at about 1:30 a.m. Friday."

We had just been there.

That's the part that stayed with me. Not just that it happened, but how close it was. How ordinary it sounded. How easily it could have been any of us — walking, talking, existing in the same spaces without thinking twice about it.

He was younger than us. Living in the same city. Doing the same things.

And suddenly, the night before felt different. The pancakes didn't just feel like pancakes anymore. The conversations didn't just feel like conversations. Everything felt more fragile. Like it existed on the edge of something we don't think about until we're forced to.

05
Frame 05
This is the IM building
35mm · Jan '26

This is the IM building.

I've worked here since the beginning of my sophomore year — August 2023. It's just a building to most people. Somewhere you swipe in, work out, leave. I never even went to the gym before this. It wasn't something I cared about, not something I saw myself being a part of.

But this place means something different to me.

I had just come back to Ann Arbor after the worst summer of my life. I was living in a house where I wasn't even on speaking terms with two out of the four people there. Every day felt heavy. I carried this constant, quiet pain from everything that had been said, everything that had happened. It didn't feel like a home. It felt like something I was stuck in.

And I kept hearing the same things over and over again. "I'll figure out a way to take away your scholarships." "I'll never pay for anything again." "You'll be stuck here forever." "You'll never be able to leave." "You're an idiot." "Be smarter." "What is wrong with you."

At some point, I stopped reacting to the words and just started believing I needed a way out. So I got a job.

My first job ever — the product of being the youngest daughter, I guess. The closest thing I could find. I didn't care what it was. It wasn't about interest, it wasn't about passion. It was about survival. It was about giving myself something, anything, that felt like control.

When I applied in June 2023 and got the job two weeks later, I sobbed. Because for the first time, I felt like I had done something for myself. Like I had created even the smallest opening to get out of a place that felt suffocating.

And somehow, this building became more than just a job. I met some of my best friends here. I had somewhere to go. Something to do. A reason to not just sit in my thoughts. It gave me structure when everything else felt unstable.

Looking back now, it doesn't feel like a small decision. It feels like the first real step I took toward finding myself.

Roll 02 — 35mm
February 2026
3 frames
Frame 01
Is there a theme here? We're back at the IM
35mm · Feb '26

Is there a theme here? We're back at the IM.

At this point, it's just… normal. A routine. I wake up, get ready for class, check the weather, and decide — do I risk it and drive to the back lot of the IM for the two-hour parking (it's always a gamble), or do I walk to the IM bus stop and head into campus that way?

It's weird how much this place has infiltrated my life.

Even now, when I'm juggling three jobs and only have time for one shift here, I still find myself walking into this building at least four times a week. Not even for work. Sometimes just to cut through to the bus stop. Sometimes to play racquetball. Sometimes to walk on the track in the back, getting lost in long conversations about anything and everything.

I am always here.

And now, somehow, my friends are too. People who had never stepped foot in this building before I started working here now treat it like it's part of their routine too. Isn't that strange?

How one person's life slowly seeps into the lives of the people around them. The places they go, the habits they build, the language they use. The good, the bad, and everything in between.

People really are a culmination of those around them.

It's funny, because my parents used to say this to me all the time growing up. "Be careful who your friends are, they'll influence you," they'd say, over and over again, in their stern Bangla.

And I used to roll my eyes. I'm my own person, I'd think. I was stubborn. Quiet, but stubborn.

And now I'm here — not that much older — and somehow, everything they said makes sense.

02
Frame 02
Don't take this the wrong way, but I'm glad someone like you is out there
35mm · Feb '26

The 2025–2026 Michigan Men's Basketball team. What a run they've had — are still having.

By this point in the year, I was burnt out. Fall semester took a lot out of me, especially with sports photography. But somehow, basketball always brings me back. There's something about it — the energy, the noise, the constant movement. The sound of shoes squeaking on the court, the crowd reacting in real time. It never really gets old.

But this game felt different.

Being a woman in sports is already hard enough. Add being young, surrounded by men decades older, constantly being looked at like you don't belong. And then add everything else — being a person of color, wearing a hijab. It's isolating. There's no other way to put it.

I got there an hour early, like I always do. I walked through the Crisler service entrance, said my usual hello to my favorite event staff member, grabbed my credential, and made my way to the media room. And like always, it was a wall of men.

And then I heard a thick French accent I immediately recognized. MG. Marc-Gregor Campredon. We started talking right away — about assignments, what we'd both been working on, how things had been going. The conversation shifted to how hard it is for young photographers to gain respect in this field.

At one point, he said something that stuck with me. "Don't take this the wrong way, but I'm glad someone like you is out there."

And for a second, everything shifted. I thought about how much I had been focusing on what felt difficult, what felt unfair, what felt exhausting. And suddenly, I saw it differently. Time is moving, and I'm in these spaces for a reason. I'm not just trying to belong. I already do.

After the first half of the game, he noticed my film camera sitting on the table. "I started with something like this," he said. "Have you taken any photos today?" "No," I told him. "Make sure you do before you leave."

And I did. In the middle of the game, in between fast-paced action shots I was supposed to be sending off to my team in Chicago, I pulled out my film camera. Something slower. Something intentional. I took one photo. And then later that month, at hockey, I did it again.

Two moments, in the middle of everything fast and overwhelming, where I chose to slow down. I love that photo. It's hanging on my living room wall now.

03
Frame 03
First day of Ramadan
35mm · Feb '26

First day of Ramadan.

I wonder if this is what Christmas feels like for most of the US. Not in the surface-level way, but in the sense that everything just feels aligned. Like there's a shared understanding in the air.

This photo is from the first taraweeh of the month. My friends and I went to the mosque together, standing shoulder to shoulder in prayer with people we know and people we don't, but somehow it doesn't matter. Ramadan does that. It brings everyone to the same place, no matter where they were before.

It doesn't matter how strong your faith was before this. It doesn't matter what you think you've done wrong. It doesn't matter how far you feel from the person you want to be. Ramadan feels like a reset.

Conversations that don't usually happen start to. People show up in ways they don't always have the space to the rest of the year. There's a kind of quiet reconnection that happens without needing to name it.

They say God locks away the devil during this month, that we're given a clean slate. And whether you interpret that literally or not, it feels real. Like for once, you're not fighting against everything at once. Like you're being given space to try again.

At the start of this year, I had so many questions for God. What are your plans for me? Why does everything feel so unclear right now? Will I be okay?

I don't even know how that sounds to someone who isn't spiritual. I've always wondered that. Not in a judgmental way, just genuinely — where do you go with those questions? Where do you put them when everything feels uncertain?

For me, they go here. Into this month. Into these nights. Into something bigger than myself.

My goal this Ramadan seems simple. I want to be someone who gives more, who reacts less, who carries themselves with more intention. And what I love about it is that I'm not doing it alone.

There's something about knowing that an entire community is trying, at the same time, to work on the worst parts of themselves. That we all collectively decide, even if just for a month, to be more patient, more generous, more aware. It makes change feel… reachable.

March 2026 — Roll 03
roll not yet developed
April 2026 — Roll 04
roll not yet developed
About this project
last semester

This is a photo narrative about my last semester of college — built frame by frame, one roll of 35mm film at a time.

The idea started simply: independence doesn't arrive in one moment. It builds slowly, through ordinary days and small decisions, through the people you find yourself around and the places you keep returning to. I wanted to document that — not the milestone moments, but the in-between ones. The ones that don't feel significant until they do.

I'm shooting one roll of 35mm film per month, from January through graduation in May. Film is part of the project's logic. It forces slowness. It means I can't take a hundred photos of the same moment and choose later — I have to decide, right then, what's worth keeping. That constraint mirrors something I've been learning about life in general this year.

Each roll becomes its own chapter. Each frame becomes its own story. The writing alongside the photos isn't meant to explain them — it's meant to sit next to them, the way a journal entry sits next to a memory.

I'm a senior at the University of Michigan. I study writing and photography. I've spent the last four years figuring out what it means to build a life that actually feels like mine — and this project is, in some ways, my attempt to document what that looks like in its final stretch.

Things aren't fully figured out yet. I think that's the point.

University of Michigan · Class of 2026
35mm film · January — May 2026
MiW Capstone Project
